title: Can be great if they would communicate...
body: Can be great if they would communicate...
pros: Weekly pay is great, and the variety of working shifts is good. 3 Months of training rather than 2 weeks.
cons: Supervisors don't communicate well with each other. Tasks will be changed quickly when they realize they caused an error, and they put the wrong people in the wrong areas. They utilize overtime a lot even when you're training. You can routinely get put into a position/job you haven't worked before, and then you will be left to figure out what you are supposed to do by yourself. Currently going through a "reassessment" of what their standards are for new hires, but not actually telling their new hires these expectations. They have a habit of firing people on their last day of probation. (ie, day 89/90 you get 90 days to get to their "standard")

title: Performance culture with workplace politic twist
body: Performance culture with workplace politic twist
pros: Not bad salary, semi flexible, good PTO
cons: If you don't play the extrovert political game you will be pushed out. If you question why something "needs" to be done, you will be pushed out. There are definitely worse companies, but this one leaves much to be desired. <3% merit raises each year. Will make you have goals that are unrealistic to give you a worse bonus or push you out. They monitor your wifi connection to mark in-office attendance and if you don't come in enough then your bonus could be impacted. Management does not have your back. It is very much a yes-man culture and if you do not tow the company line you will be miserable.

title: Back to only caring about the bottom line
body: Back to only caring about the bottom line
pros: Healthcare and 401k matching are very competitive
cons: There is no stability in Unilever's future. The company has had 3 restructurings in the past 7 years, and is on its 4th CEO in the same time. The company is lost, and have decided to now shift their focus from employee wellbeing to the bottom line. More layoffs are coming, and if the board has their way the company will be split it multiple small groups. They have also shifted how they rate employees in a way that will offer them less in bonus payouts. Unilever pretends to be agile and that they can move with the market, but they are bogged down in way to much process from within, they cannot get out of their own way. While their policies say hybrid, from within the company they are very clear they want everyone in the office all the time.

title: A Company in Decline
pros: Relaxed work culture. good vacations. fun brands. many interesting locations
cons: A failing work culture. A half decade of focusing on "brands with purpose" has resulted in an inefficient, unmotivated, and overworked workforce. Leadership has clearly been more focused on improving how they look to the media than actually driving growth and building a culture where employees feel like they are working towards something together. Unless you are at the end of your career and looking for a place to float through your final working years, you're better off developing your skills and staying competitive in your career at a company that's actually developing their employees.

body: They don't walk the talk when it comes to wellbeing
pros: Unilever is a giant organization that offers great opportunity to learn and grow and will make you highly marketable for your next move.. If your career is what is most important at this stage in your life than this might be the place for you.
cons: Despite talking a good game about wellbeing and culture, the company is unrealistically demanding and there is an expectation that you will drop everything to work around the clock. There is even a bit of hero worship for those who take 3am calls for global meetings, work on vacations and holidays, etc. If you want to have a balanced life avoid at all costs

title: Phenomenal Training - Job depends greatly on category/leader
body: Phenomenal Training - Job depends greatly on category/leader
pros: Seriously phenomenal marketing training... you will know more about strategy than the majority of your future colleagues. The brands are wonderful and diverse. Where else can you work on Dove and then go work on Ben & Jerry's or Olly's? The actual job depends 1000% on your manager and category.
cons: The autonomy & love for the job will depend upon the category you are in and your leader. If you are on Dove, you will have a bigger marketing budget than anyone else, but you will have a team of 40 marketers and have to deal with a matrix of leaders around the globe that all play brand manager. A Sr Manager on Dove has very little autonomy, but your career is set with Dove on your resume. If you are on Breyers ice cream, you literally run the entire business as a Sr Manager as its a local business and your Director runs 6 ice cream brands.

title: Good for a big company, not great for career development
body: Good for a big company, not great for career development
pros: * Company truly cares about making a positive impact on the world * Top level leadership appears to truly care about employees * Great brands to work on * Work/life balance and WFH flexibility (even pre-COVID) * Good benefits (~5 months maternity) * Good balance of team collaboration and individual self-managed work * Great experience/never gets boring, as you are encouraged to switch roles every 2 years * Handling COVID response fairly well and understanding of employees' home situations (have been highly communicative)
cons: * Titles not aligned to competitors in the industry (i.e. Assoc. Managers at Unilever could be Sr. Managers or Directors elsewhere) * Next to impossible to get promoted (at all levels). Promotions within departments are based on "who's next on the list," i.e. who has been there the longest, rather than who's performance and total experience (including outside Unilever) best prepares them for a promotion. * Middle management (Directors) often have little to no people-management experience and are not good at managing teams * Work experience outside of UL doesn't count/doesn't matter since people often stay here for so long * Lots of top-down micromanagement/lack of trust in teams to make decisions

title: Shady, Unethical, and Fake Leadership
pros: Beautiful workspace, free ice cream, great perks, some people are genuinely good people (though many of the best have left)
cons: The leadership is horrible! They talk about "Every Voice Matters" but it's totally fake from the top down. They don't care about diversity and inclusion - it's all a numbers game. I honestly believe some of them are sociopaths, especially in HR and Legal. I personally witnessed truly abhorrent behavior and when several people complained to the legal department, they did nothing except cover it up. Shameful! 80+ hour work weeks with no relief, including weekends and holidays; very few people are set up for success. Leadership says they will help you if you come to them, but when you come to them, they do nothing except penalize you. Much of the work is ego-driven and has no value. There's a reason the rate of people of color leaving is so high - the Diversity & Inclusion program is a total joke. Favoritism is rampant. Bias runs unchecked.

title: Beware of Health Insurance
pros: Good company vision. Great campus
cons: If you have a spouse or a family member that requires special medical need or attention, I'd strongly recommend you to look elsewhere. Ever since UL changed coverage provider from UHC to Cigna, the healthcare coverage has been awful. I'm spending inordinate amount of time dealing with medical claims denials of my family member for basic medical needs. It's stressful enough being a caretaker for your loved ones but to be caught between the two conglomerates puts a whole new meaning to being "squeezed out." I'm sure UL is saving lots of money from switching UHC to Cigna, but this isn't coming from reducing health care costs or signing a better deal. These savings are directly paid for by the UL employees and families who are being denied essential medical coverage and are resorting to paying out of pocket.

cons: Organization is very complex with difficulty landing accountability with someone.

title: Development Oppurtunities
pros: Great Development opportunities, Agile and Flexible working culture, emphasis on diversity, great people to work with
cons: Large organization comes with its own bureaucracy

title: It's okay. Not so good for manpower.
body: It's okay. Not so good for manpower.
pros: Alot of overtime when on. Easy job. Great bosses.
cons: If you not hourly flexible don't take the job.

title: HRBP
pros: Unilever is an amazing Corporate Citizen!
cons: Our brands are well known but Unilever as a company is less recognizable

title: Good but not Great company
pros: Ok on work/life balance and fair pay. They also push to challenge you in different roles and offer different courses when needed.
cons: Severe lack of training in some areas

title: Buyer Beware
pros: Good agile culture (if your manager will allow it), flexible work environment, great learning with intelligent people.
cons: -no development -lack of diversity -no support from HR or many support function -irrational leadership (HR/Comms) - stay far away from them!!!! I felt harrassed and intimidated by my manager. When I brought it to HR, they made the problem worse by going to him and letting him know who said it. The turnover rate of employee is staggering particularly in HR and Communications but overall in the entire company. Get in, get the experience, and get out. Associates and Manager: Do not stay as they will string you along with implications of promotion and recognition, but you will lose a lot of your earning potential in this time.

title: Job facts
pros: For me,close to home , 2-2-3 schedule gives you plenty of days off .Very fair work environment.I have seen several employees transition from production floor to management once they attained their degree,very diverse work force including management.Unilever does not tolerate unprofessional and unsafe behavior will terminate violators so it is a safe work environment.
cons: 12 hour shifts can be hard on your feet and lower back ,line shutdowns cut overtime for those of us who want it.

title: Growth potential
pros: Great company with pleasant professional people to work with. People given opportunity to express their ideas and considered. Off hands management at times can be both a plus and negative.
cons: Very demanding at times and loosely managed periodically. Hands off management can increase stress at times when slow to respond to underlying issues until at times becomes a top priority and always in fire fighting mode.

title: Amazing People and Purpose and an Awesome Place to Work!!
body: Amazing People and Purpose and an Awesome Place to Work!!
pros: The list of reasons why Unilever is the best place to work is way too long. So below are just a few: Purpose â UL realizes the value of people who have a purpose as much as our brands with a purpose. People â the organization cares about their employees as much as they do customers and consumers. Also the people who work at UL are smart, nice and most importantly authentic. Brands â It is really an awesome feeling to let people know you work for this amazing company Unilever, and âOh by the way, they make Dove, Hellmannâs, Ben & Jerryâsâ. Flexibility â agile work environment. Being able to get your job done from wherever, whenever. Empowerment â being encouraged to own and make decisions and knowing that the leaders have your back. and many more...
cons: With all large organization, process can get in the way of being able to get things done quickly. Unilever has done a tremendous job over the past few years of tackling this barrier by changing the way we work. This has removed a lot of the complexity which has allowed things to be done quicker and simpler.

cons: This company has no Cons.

title: Great exposure, too rigid and micromanaged
body: Great exposure, too rigid and micromanaged
pros: Company Name Wonderful products in our category Large organization that believes in sustainability - very proud of that fact Complex projects Experience will take you on for other opportunities that will grow your career
cons: Truly depends on the team and I think overall Unilever is terrific but I decided to leave for the following reasons primarily due to my department culture: Limited work life balance Being recognized for After the fact and not in the moment Aggressively micromanaged, little trust Foul language in the office sometimes lack of visibility getting to engage with senior leaders Trying to make the department goals your overall career ambitions Just chaotic, lack of direction

title: Maintenance Techician
pros: Good pay and benefits. Nice facility
cons: Leadership doesn't listen to the people on the floor. They see a problem and armchair quarterback an idea and shove it down your throat. If you do it and it fails, then its more work and frustration on you. If you challenge them and hold your ground with your solution, then you have a bad attitude.

title: Great company to work for
pros: No micromanagement Great employees I had a good time
cons: No cons I had a great time getting to meet new people

title: Review of Unilever US
pros: Great work life balance, ability to work remotely, get to work on some of the industry's biggest brands, great access to resources
cons: terrible middle managers (brand managers) who throw their direct reports under the bus, afraid to say no or push back on Brand Directors who suggest a change in direction (don't even bother to attempt an explanation for a different approach even when previously aligned with the rest of the team), middle managers resort to making up performance issues even though emails and presentations exist to prove otherwise - and when an attempt at singling out 'performance issue X' fails, miraculously yet another 'performance issue Y' is inserted into the mix. The environment this foster is toxic, full of mistrust and completely lacking in respect and integrity.

title: Unilever
pros: Company fully supports the idea that social, economical and environmental responsibility positively impact the bottom-line. Great energy always looking to improve.
cons: Silo behaviors. Current organizational confusion.

title: Manufacturing Supervisor
pros: Opportunities if the favoritism favors you. Lots of resources due to company size.
cons: Massive favoritism in management, and HR doesn't do much to help out even when major issues are presented. Saw a couple cases of sexual harassment go nowhere.

title: Local facility needs serious help, but globally, the company is strong
body: Local facility needs serious help, but globally, the company is strong
pros: Get to work for global leader in manufacturing. Pay is reasonable, not great. Benefits for salary workers are good. Other employers will throw a net around you after working at Unilever.
cons: Ridiculous working schedule. Little opportunity to grow from within the organization. Large disconnect between reality and plans on paper. For highly engineered equipment, little support is given to following through with engineered ideas. No value in training or developing individuals.

title: Change is the constant
pros: Great Business acumen in massive consumer goods, great benefits( health, bonus, etc), agile, flexifriday, stability, brands with purpose, Monthly giveaways,
cons: Opportunity for a clear define and straight processes, opportunity for clear role objectives, bureaucratic for the decision making process, high personnel rotations

title: Challenging organization and culture - US FTEs reduction
body: Challenging organization and culture - US FTEs reduction
pros: View of a large global organization Broad cultural experience Good pay scale compared to other companies of the same size
cons: Reducing US jobs for several years in exchange for regions with cheaper labor costs. Limited to no opportunities for US employees who want to move up. Early and/or late US work hours depend on global teams time zone coordination Challenging cultural ways of working Slow to move to new technologies for production due to vast differences in regions - One size does not fit all

title: Great place to work
pros: Giving a great opportunity to retire to a wonderful place
cons: They really should do more back ground checks on the managers they place on the production floors, some are not really manager material.

title: Deduction Analyst
pros: Fair, Good work life balance
cons: Only want to promote younger candidates

title: Customer Service Analyst
pros: Ability to work from home; flexible hours; new, modern building with game room and free ice cream; sit/stand desks with ergonomic chairs; friendly co-workers; half-days on Friday during summer; end of year bonus; good company values; holiday party
cons: End-of-year calibration where managers are required to assign each worker a ranking that operates on a bell curve, resulting in good workers being let go for "low performance" and causing employees to work against each other to get ahead; usually a round of layoffs every 2-3 years; shifting focus of company objectives from year to year; only paid for 37.5 hours a week when usually working 40+, inconsistent training/onboarding for new hires; cancelling holiday party or other social events due to budget cuts; difficult to get promoted to management level unless part of Future Leaders program or ex-military

title: Ben & Jerry's St. Albans, VT
pros: Full time job & management passes out beer during company community day! Also, at times the maintenance shop is more like a barroom.
cons: 1% across the board raise for hourly employees while management receives large bonuses. Interoffice affairs are common place & appear to be the only way to move up the ladder. Place has gone down hill under the direction of the current factory director in VT. Benefits are constantly taken away & headcount is being cut causing people to work in unsafe conditions. Yet management threatens people with termination if they get hurt. Accidents are hidden & hourly employees work in a hostile fear driven environment

title: Long Term Trend is Negative; Do Not Believe all the Hype
body: Long Term Trend is Negative; Do Not Believe all the Hype

Many people actually believe in Unileverâs social and sustainability strategy, and act accordingly. The majority of people behave ethically and politely. Itâs a great place to build up your skills quickly, and in some parts of the world, having Unilever on your CV is still meaningful

title: Maybe a good stop but not good for the long term, get in and get out.
body: Maybe a good stop but not good for the long term, get in and get out.
pros: Products, some of the people. Products and some of the people since I need 20 words and there aren't 20 pros.
cons: Leadership (lack of), favoritism, have to be in the club to survive comfortably. Some of the people. Pay (lack of). Does not care about the people. Safety is important until it interferes with production.

title: Unilever
pros: Work life balance Lots of career opportunities Supports further education
cons: Lower average salary compared to regional and national averages

title: Going downhill fast
pros: - Flexible/agile work hours depending on your team - Company has a great social mission, unfortunately it doesn't translate to the - Working on huge well known brands - A lot is asked from you which gives you an opportunity to learn a lot in a short amount of time
cons: The company is going downhill fast, I wouldn't return unless they do some serious overhauling on their business strategy. Short sighted cost savings initiatives are killing the culture and will continue to drive employees to leave. With all the opportunities in the NYC area, you would be a fool to stay with the sinking ship. - Terrible retention; probably due to everyone having to double hat with no promotions or increase in salary - Not much room for advancement. Management expects you to stick around in the same role with negligable raises for years, even if you are doing a good job - Pay is not great for NYC area. Company will short change and in some cases stiff you on your already small bonus - They make employees that live in NYC pay to ride the shuttle to work

title: Do Not Recommend

title: review from a designated "high performer"

title: Culture is going downhill fast
pros: agile working, aspirational external goals set by ceo...that's really about it
cons: no opportunity for advancement, political, culture is terrible and creates nasty and unhealthy competition causing people to leave, everyone double hats because of so many vacancies but not compensated or rewarded at all. people are beyond frustrated or have already left and are loving their new companies. also maybe give health benefits if not bigger salaries/ bonuses.

title: SC Manager
pros: Great benefits, very community oriented and strong focus on sustainability
cons: Currently volatile - many layoffs

title: Values-Driven company
pros: This company is very clear on its social mission and actually walks the talk. It champions sustainable living, diversity, wellness and it trickles down in the day to day discussions in the company. Highly competitive environment that allows for growth opportunities.
cons: Unilever is not the biggest payers out there but the benefits more than compensates for it.

title: Rates Employees on Personalities Rather than Results
body: Rates Employees on Personalities Rather than Results
pros: Benefits and agile working environment Hires talent (for the most part) Invests time and money in developing people Invests in a lot of team building activities and events Promotes culture where you're in charge of your own project; no one will present your work but you Most managers push you outside your comfort zone and encourage you to do your best
cons: If one Sr. Manager or Director doesn't like you/your personality, you're out of there! I've witnessed several employees being told they're not "outgoing enough" or "too confident" for Unilever. Further, Unilever does what they call a 360 Feedback where your Manager asks your peers/business partners to rate you. So, if you've had an difference of opinion with one of your business partners, they may negatively assess your abilities in this Feedback. This will surely make its way into your review; basically, it's the art of making people like you. In most companies, if you're underperforming, your manager would give you at least a few warnings and perhaps help you establish a roadmap for improvement. Not here, my friend. At Unilever, they will blindsight you with a rating that signals "underperformance" at the end of the year and there's absolutely nothing you can do about it.

title: Major change in medical benefits this year.
body: Major change in medical benefits this year.
pros: Great environment to work in. Flexible work hours. Recently made a change to 3 weeks paid vacay for new hires.
cons: Upward mobility is non existent. Also the benefits have really taken a turn for the worse. Low salary compared to industry average. Out sourcing NA domestic jobs.

title: This job had me considering suicide.
body: This job had me considering suicide.
pros: They'll pay you a decent wage, so you can afford a nice apartment to go cry in for six hours before you have to be back for another 12 hour shift. And another. And another. Continue for 6 months until you give up on life.
cons: Worked 80 hours a week. NO time for a life at home. Erratic, constantly changing hours. days off are few and far between. They will literally work you to death without any remorse. I was having thoughts of self-harm and suicide by the time I was able to get away from this terrible place.

title: Unilever USA - Services
pros: Agile working, great people, care about the environment, challenging projects and opportunities
cons: Constant change sometimes 2-3 reorgs per year, massive move recently to outsource many US-based global roles to low-cost countries

title: operator
pros: I'm hard pressed to find 20 words to say good things about Unilever. The people who share the same treatment as each other is about it. You can lean on each other to help you through the ungodly amount of hours, exhausting work, constant stress.

title: former employee
pros: Great people...solid company with amazing social/environmental missions across the world.
cons: Don't expect to be promoted. It is very challenging for someone who wants to climb. You can do anything there and it is supported, but you will very rarely see someone move to a higher position.

title: In general
pros: Casual environment, nice co-workers,family first values
cons: Constantly having re-orgs and company wide layoffs. Was there for ten years and every year there was one if not two rounds of layoffs. No way to live. The latest large layoff was due to lack of growth. Also, their benefits are no longer that great or competitive as they once were.

title: Nice Place
pros: Excellent benefits with great people. Work life balance is excellent and management cares. Help people grow and learn. Highly recommended.
cons: Not enough upward mobility for young employees who want to move up. Hard to get ahead at the company, but can be done.

title: Great Company
pros: I found Unilever to be a great company to work for. The people and atmosphere are friendly and the company listens to and respects their employees and their feedback. They offer a lot of benefits (i.e. stock options, 401k, company store, fitness center...) and support a work/life balance.
cons: My department was located in a different building and sometimes we felt isolated from the rest of the company, however, they are in the process of incorporating all departments into one building which will resolve this issue.

title: Biased HR & Pro managers policies
pros: The overall work culture is good..good work life balance however most of the facilities & policies are for managers. WL1 has slow or no growth...the performance ratings are normalized which is a big negative...women employees joining back from MAT leave have to struggle to save their jobs as there is no support or clarity on their roles either from LM or HR.HR is biased & is only for managers...due to which many of them had to leave their jobs
cons: HR is biased & does not support WL1. They don't have growth plan.

title: Operator

cons: I havent come across any cons

title: Great company

cons: Very political environment, it is very much about who you know and having someone up the ladder advocating for you. Global teams require working very much off hours. Agile working offsets this but there are times 11PM calls are followed up by 530AM calls the next morning depending on the region you are working with.

title: Overall Review

cons: Not too much movement between departments

title: Great, if you can play the game.
body: Great, if you can play the game.
pros: Very flexible with working from home, awesome work/life balance. A lot of great people to work with.
cons: Politics is everywhere but this is the worst I've seen it. Your career is fully dependent on having the right Manager or your ability to play the game. Those that do get promoted are often excellent at managing up but don't have the respect from their peers or people at lower levels.

title: Brand Manager
pros: Amazing company and people. Best company I have ever worked for.
cons: Slow to promote and career is too dependent on the manager.

title: Senior Scientist
pros: Good work life balance, good benefits, great co-workers. Overall a really good company to work for - no major issues any fortune 500 doesn't have.
cons: A lot of people involved in decision making. Trouble getting things to the market quickly. Global roles are difficult on normal working hours.

title: Great people

I worked here for many years and everyone of them was enjoyable. I wish I had not left. Really smart people everywhere.
cons: No real negatives. For a company of this size to run as well as it does is amazing.

title: Great place to start your career
body: Great place to start your career
pros: A great place to start your career. You are surrounded by talented people who will become mentors and friends. Heavy emphasis on having your MBA when it comes to marketing. You have a lot of resources at such a big company.
cons: Very competitive to move up in the organization, a lot of bottle necks. Lots of structural changes within the company (we had an reorganization 3 times in 3 years).

title: Great Company Culture
pros: -Positive and supportive company culture -Opportunities for growth and development -Ability to move cross-functionally -Global company with highly ranked supply chain, well known brands, involved in giving back and making decisions that increase sustainability living -For the most part, strong processes in place -Competitive pay with annual bonus and salary increases available (mostly based on performance) -Great benefits (6% 401k match, 16 week maternity leave, 3 weeks vacation per year agile working environment and ways to gain health care discounts)
cons: -Some departments can be highly political, slightly tough environment if you aren't used to it -Some teams can feel like silos -As with any large company, re-organizations every couple years -Frequent hiring freezes which can add more work to your plate if a role cannot be quickly backfilled or a potential delay in your moving to next role

title: IT-Manager
pros: Flexible work arrangement, agile workplace, decent salary/bonuses and pretty good benefits. Some nice perks (company store, free products every now and then, gym, employee appreciation days, good training and conference opportunities).
cons: If in a global role, big chances you will have to do some non business hours (i.e. conference calls at 6am or 7pm). Also some international travel might be expected of you (some see this as a pro I guess).

title: IT was the BEST of TIMES, IT was the WORST of TIMES
body: IT was the BEST of TIMES, IT was the WORST of TIMES
pros: Smart young people, like shiny pennies in the sun; Great facilities and benefits, goal oriented long term project targets, some of the nicest people I have ever worked with. Relative stability as a true giant of commerce. Few, if any, fist pounders
cons: Exists as a communist state stifling most creativity. There were meetings to the 3rd power of pi focused on triviality. As with most communist regimes, party members; sorry, employees, worry about the limited chance to move up the food chain but find it better to just be quiet. Zero risk takers with zero personalities. Lunch should not be the highlight of your work day.

body: Get company to work for/lots of good benefits

body: Amazing employees and free ice cream!

Every single person I've met was easy to approach and had a smile on their face. The fact that I'm making the same products I use to enjoy as a kid. Amazing pay, vacation, personal holidays, inside gym, company store, and inside gym. Free ice cream everyday but you're not allowed to leave with it :(
cons: We only get 2 days a month and it strains your body a bit. It sometimes makes it worth it because we get time and a half and double pay. We're not allowed to take home ice cream which I don't understand, because they put pint and quart sizes in there and I would like to take what I started.

title: Director
pros: The company is making strides in driving the sustainability agenda for itself, and by following companies. Training is top-tier, and some of the smartest people in the industry work here. Pay and benefits are decent.
cons: For a US citizen the challenges are many. Too many mid t0 senior level positions are filled by foreign expats (that are cheaper, and they do understand the most complex and advanced market in the world), which makes promotion difficult for US citizens. Senior leaders bring in their own people. Also the US system relies on MBAs, which makes it difficult for US citizens to work abroad in this global corporation. The starting pay scale for a US MBA Associate is more than a Director makes in many other markets. Nowhere else wants to pay for "an expensive American", making the wonderful opportunity for a global career limiting for a US citizen. Also- work/life balance is a struggle. The company is competitive and forces bell curve calibration for bonus and merit increase annually. Between this and heavy work loads make the work/life balance tough if you want to succeed.

cons: Salary insurance not the best.

body: Great for Getting Experience, High Amount of Change

Very little autonomy to be innovative or creative. Senior leadership is almost all international. Very few Americans at the top.

cons: Too much politics in the New Jersey Office.

title: Unilever... A Great Place to Work!
body: Unilever... A Great Place to Work!
pros: Unilever is an excellent company with high ethics who strongly supports diversity, sustainability and work life balance. Company has a great culture which really fosters team work.
cons: Unilever has limited locations in the United States.

title: Director
pros: Global company of scale that is "walking the talk" in terms of sustainability and positive community/social impact. Some opportunity to be entrepeneurial (intrapeneurial) despite its size. Smart, energetic colleagues. Annual employee feedback / pulse check is shared and )mostly) acted upon.
cons: Scope of roles expanded to unsustainable point for those wishing to maintain quality of life. Value of talent not always evident in management decisions (related to benefits, policy, restructuring or reward).

title: Great Place to Work
pros: Global scalable operations with a high performance culture and corporate values. Equal opportunity employer
cons: Increased complexity due to size and structure of operations

title: HRBP
pros: Great company, great culture, great benefits. Everybody has a 'can-do' & positive mentality. Working with smart people really energizes me. The Unilever Sustainable Living Plan is embedded in many of the employee's values. Although the company is big, it tries to stay ahead of it's competition and does not shut it's eyes for changes in the marketplace. Get a lot of room for growth from day1!
cons: As it is a very big organization sometimes you need to find your own way in the complexity. As the company tries to stay ahead of it's competition (which is great!) sometimes too many changes going on.

title: Big organization
pros: Lot of directions you can take. Many great people to learn from. If you have personal goals you can make it in this size of an organization. Money is also good for the area.
cons: With a big organization you can be looked over. You have to make things happen on your own. No one is going to hold your hand and show you the way.

title: Supply Planner
pros: Great place to work - the benefits are great, pay is really good, Agile working is a great perk to have.
cons: It is a fast paced environment, can be stressful at times. Very results oriented - Management expects measureable results.

title: Work hard, play hard, have fun at Unilever
body: Work hard, play hard, have fun at Unilever
pros: Work is challenging and fulfilling Freedom to lead and execute projects No micromanagement Purpose-driven business agenda Culture of trust and "I have your back" Fun onsite and offsite activities to engage employees Excellent benefits, beyond the norm Flexible and agile working environment HR leadership is crazy innovative and forward-thinking Commitment to gender parity
cons: Employees who are not self-motivated will struggle Email volumes are hard to manage There is a lot of system simplification left to do (which is also a pro if you're looking for an opportunity to make an impact)

title: Assistant Manager Portfolio Procurement
pros: Opportunity for internal mobility flexibility to work from home when needed company store comprehensive benefits package including tuition reimbursement (pending approval), FSA, full healthcare, annual bonus (payout varies by work level)
cons: global isn't always in touch with regional strategy and operational capabilities very bureaucratic a lot of talk about career development but no follow through over burdened with process

title: Requirements Planner
pros: Diversity and Inclusion in the workplace
cons: Nothing outside of basic corporate America in terms of restructures.

cons: no say in if you will move to another location. competitive work environment which can be difficult at times.

title: Unilever
pros: Unilever pays well and there is lots of opportunities for growth. I have been happy with the benefits packaging including the 401k. They have been flexible with me on my hours.
cons: The demands are high on time and productivity, which on it's own wouldn't be bad. However, resources required to meet the demands are often scarce and there are many, many competing priorities and leadership is disconnected from the rank and file.

title: Good company to work for

title: This is a great company to work for because they invest in their employees.
body: This is a great company to work for because they invest in their employees.
pros: This is a great company to work for because they invest in their employees, are serious about their sustainability program (USLP), and have so many different opportunities that you'll always find a new challenge to tackle.
cons: They are very process oriented. Great because you minimize risks and failures. Tough because you are not organized to move fast and end up being a follower more often than leader.

title: Brand Building

cons: Everyone is stressed out and has too much on their plate

title: Not the place to work at! high turnover! Lies!
body: Not the place to work at! high turnover! Lies!
pros: Pay. Very close to home.
cons: Benefits not the best, Union environment, management is out of place. Treat you with alot of disrespect. Training is not structured and old timers there are not willing to train you. They give you attitudes and everyone hates working there due to the forcing and understaffed place. This place has a very high turnover of employees! They lied about my pay, my schedules, paycheck was never correct ever since I got hired at the place.

title: They sued a colleague after laying him off after years over 10 of service and high calibrating performance every year
body: They sued a colleague after laying him off after years over 10 of service and high calibrating performance every year
pros: None that I can think of. It was hell.

title: Two month review
pros: Constantly moving so you never feel tired or bored. Figuring out the most efficient ways to get tasks accomplished is somewhat edifying. They're good about working around your schedule and benefits are decent. Everyone is friendly.
cons: They're currently spread thin so it feels like you're constantly hustling to get everything done. No time to slow down and investigate issues with their product on engineering side.

title: Cold environment; no warm and fuzzy!
body: Cold environment; no warm and fuzzy!
pros: The people you work with are great and are the only thing keeping this place together. Good benefits and more flexibility to work from home than other companies.
cons: More and more little things that make a big impact to employees keep getting cut! No more holiday lunch offered; which was an annual thing and gave the opportunity for teams to break away and have a nice meal together. No holiday gather period! At least the year before they allowed us to do something in the new year but now it's nothing! Let's just say if it wasn't for the ppl it was a cold holiday season in the office and I'm not looking forward this year!

title: Unilever is a good place to start a career right out of school. I will not recommend for mid-career hires.
body: Unilever is a good place to start a career right out of school. I will not recommend for mid-career hires.
pros: - Very good healthcare benefits in comparison to sister companies - Great place to launch a technical or business career right out of college or business school - There is an emphasis on work/life balance and an agile work environment - Global footprint and international career opportunities for those interested
cons: - Very political performance review process. It get's worse as you progress into management positions. It becomes all about "who knows you" as opposed to what you have accomplished. - In recent years Unilever has significantly decreased spending on employee training and development. This has resulted in fewer internal promotions and more recruitment of external candidates into management positions. - A very bureaucratic environment and a centralized organizational structure that slows down decision making. - In the last decade most of Unilever's growth has been through acquisitions. It lacks the entrepreneurial spirit to quickly launch new innovations and to grow organically.

cons: there are no cons. because what people call cons i see it as challenge that needs my attention.

title: Bad Place to work
pros: Location, updated office, company store
cons: Unilever will chew you up and spit you out. They do not care at all how much dedication you have given to the company, If you identify an issue and present it with evidence to HR they will sweep it under a rug quickly, not help you and let the issue, turn it on you, take all your documentation, do nothing about it, and then lay you off and walk you out regardless of your dedication and performance.. No one cares about how valuable your skills are here, its all social and not performance related. If you like that, apply today!!!

title: Customer Development
pros: People are great- some of the best i've ever had the pleasure of working with.
cons: Absolutely decimated the employee compensation and benefits over the past 2yrs. Cut bonuses by 33%, annual raise will now be a flat 0%-1% regardless of performance, medical coverage is terrible and will cost you a fortune. Notorious for screwing the employee- not only do they charge you $200/month for your company car, but in 2015 a handful of employees did not have this fee deducted due to an HR error...they are going after the employee to pay back the fee rather than overlooking it since it was a Unilever issue. In May 2014 a huge reorg resulted in mass layoffs and a large number of demotions so the comany could cut costs...the 2 weeks later they announced a massive renovation to headquarters com,plete with electric car ports, a botanical garden, and many other bells and whistles......compliments of the employees who were let go....real classy Unilever.

title: Great Brands - Bogged Down and Internal Politics
body: Great Brands - Bogged Down and Internal Politics
pros: You get a chance to work with great, well know brands. The agile work environment gives you flexibility depending on your manager - most people work from home on Fridays. It's not a cutthroat environment like some companies.
cons: There seems to be a shift in culture where people now find reasons NOT to help each other. Internal politics are also more in play. On a global basis, each region/cluster head is more worried about building their own empire vs doing what is right for the company as a whole. We like to talk about "One Unilever" but it's just talk. Processes are too complex and decision making is still way too slow despite efforts to simplify. Restructuring takes place about every 24 months, which just throws things into chaos. People are also rotated out of roles every 12-18 months, which is too fast to really gain experience from a role. There is very little room to move into a Director role. You basically have to wait until someone leaves, which never happens.
